a hamlet in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

oldenzijl.e:
* (village) First attested as oldazile in 1358. Compound of oud (“old”) and zijl (“discharge sluice”).
* (hamlet) Compound of oud (“old”) and zijl (“discharge sluice”).

s.def: a village in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

onderdendam.e: First attested as ulderna domme in 1252. Compound of Old Frisian *Ulderna (a surname) or *Ullerna (a surname) and dam (“dam”). Compare Onderwierum.

s.def: a village in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

oosternieland.e: First attested as den nyen lande in 1448. From ooster- (“eastern, east-”) and nieuwland (“newly reclaimed land”). The element ooster was added to distinguish the settlement from Westernieland.

s.def: a village in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

oudeschip.e: First attested as het Oude-Schip in 1848. Compound of oud (“old”) and schip (“ship”). According to local history, the settlement sprang up around a beached ship.

s.def: a hamlet in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

onderwierum.e: First attested as uldernawerum in 1378. Likely a compound of a family name and the dative plural form of Old Frisian weer (“artificial mound used for habitation”). Compare Onderdendam.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

oostervalge.e: Attested as Ooster Valge in 1867. From ooster- (“eastern, east-”) and dialectal valg (“tilled land”). The element ooster was added to distinguish the toponym from Westervalge.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

obergum.e: First attested as obergon in the 10th or 11th century. Etymology uncertain. Possibly a compound of a personal name and either Old Frisian berg (“hill, elevated place”) or hem (“home, settlement”). Formerly an independent village.

s.def: a village in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

oldekerk.e: First attested as aeldakerka in 1392. Compound of Old Frisian ald (“old”) and kerk (“church”). The element ald was added to distinguish the settlement from Niekerk.

s.def: a village in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

oldehove.e:
* (Groningen) First attested as oldenhove in 1392. Compound of Middle Dutch out (“old”) and the dative singular form of hof (“farmstead, homestead”). See also Dutch Low Saxon Ollehoof.
* (Friesland) Compound of oud (“old”) and hof (“farmstead, homestead”). Formerly an independent village.

s.def: a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

okswerd.e: First attested as oxwerderzijl in 1422. Compound of Old Frisian Okke (a personal name) or oxa (“ox, castrated bull”) and either a term cognate to Old Dutch werd (“land next to water, riverine island”) or were (“plot of land, farmstead”).

s.def: a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

oosterzand.e: First attested as t Ooster Sand in 1781. From ooster- (“eastern, east-”) + zand (“sand”). The toponym contrasts with Westerzand.

s.def: a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

oostindië.e: First attested as de Oostindiën in 1839. Derived from Oost-Indië (“East Indies”).

s.def: a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

oostum.e: First attested as astnem [read: asthem] in the 11th century. Compound of Old Frisian āst (“east, eastern-”) and heem (“home, settlement”).

oostdongeradeel.e: Borrowed from West Frisian East-Dongeradiel, a compound of east (“east-, eastern”) and the toponym Dongeradiel.

ophuis.e: First attested as Ophuijs in 1543. Calque of West Frisian Ophûs, a compound of op (“up-, elevated”) and hûs (“house”).

s.def: a hamlet in Achtkarspelen, Friesland, Netherlands

opperkooten.e: Calque of West Frisian Opperkoaten, a compound of opper (“upper, up-”) and the toponym De Koaten.

s.def: a village in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

oosternijkerk.e: First attested as nova ecclesia in 1224. Calque of West Frisian Easternijtsjerk, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian nija (“new”) and tzercka (“church”). The element easter (“eastern, east-”) was added later to distinguish the settlement from Westernijkerk.

Compound of an otherwise unknown toponym *Oostum (derived in turn from Old Frisian āst (“east-, eastern”) and hēm (“home, settlement”)) and hern (“corner of a levee”). The Dutch version of the toponym derives from a superseded Frisian name; th...

oudwoude.e: First attested as oldewolde in 1444. Calque of West Frisian Aldwâld, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian ald (“old”) and wâld (“forest”).

s.def: a hamlet in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

oosterbeintum.e: First attested as osterbinehheim in 944. Calque of West Frisian Easterbeintum, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian aster (“eastern, east-”), bint (“moor grass, Molinia sp.”) and hēm (“home, settlement”).

s.def: a hamlet in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

oudterp.e: First attested as older torp and alderterp in the 16th century. Calque of West Frisian Ald Terp, derived from âld (“old”) and terp (“artificial mound used for habitation”).

s.def: a hamlet in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

oudwouderzijlen.e: Calque of West Frisian Aldwâldmersyl, derived from a compound of the toponym Aldwâld and syl (“discharge sluice”).

oostermeer.e: First attested as oestmeer in 1447. Calque of West Frisian Eastermar, a compound of Old Frisian āster (“eastern, east-”) and mar (“lake”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Tytsjerksteradiel, Friesland, Netherlands

oenkerk.e: First attested as ontzerka in 1408. Borrowed from West Frisian Oentsjerk, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian Oene (a personal name) and tzercka (“church”).

oudkerk.e: First attested as aldakerka in 1421. Calque of West Frisian Aldtsjerk, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian alda (“old”) and tzercka (“church”).

s.def: a village in Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands

oosterlittens.e: Potentially attested as lechinke [read: lethinge] in the second half of the 13th century, attested with certainty as aesterletzenze in 1402. oude leije.e: First attested as op ter leya in 1466. Calque of West Frisian Alde Leie, derived in turn from Old Frisian ald (“old”) and leije (“excavated or canalised watercourse”).

opeinde.e: First attested as wp an endde in 1439. Derived from Middle Dutch opeinde (“upper end”).

opperburen.e: First attested as d' Opperbuiren in 1718. Calque of West Frisian Opperbuorren, a compound of opper (“upper”) and the plural form of buer (“house, cottage”).

olterterp.e: First attested as utrathorp in 1315. Borrowed from West Frisian Olterterp, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian utra (“outer, outward”) and thorp (“village”). The second element was gradually reinterpreted as terp (“artificial mound used for habitation”). The toponym contrasts with Uret...

oosterend.e:

* (Terschelling) First attested as apud Sanctum Nycolaum in 1296. Compound of ooster- (“eastern, east-”) and end (“end, edge”)....

oud beets.e: First attested as beke in 1315. Borrowed from West Frisian Ald Beets, derived in turn from âld (“old”) and Old Frisian bitze (“brook, stream”). The element âld was added to distinguish the settlement from Nij Beets.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Heerenveen, Friesland, Netherlands

oldeboorn.e: First attested as bornde in 1243. Borrowed from West Frisian Aldeboarn, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian ald (“old”) and the hydronym Boarn.

s.def: a hamlet in Heerenveen, Friesland, Netherlands

oude schouw.e: First attested as de Oude-Schouw in 1840. Calque of West Frisian Aldskou, a compound of âld (“old”) and skou (“ferry”). Originally the name of an inn.

oosterboorn.e: First attested as Oosterboorn in 1846. Calque of West Frisian Easterboarn, compound of easter (“eastern”) and the hydronym Boarn.

s.def: a village in Heerenveen, Friesland, Netherlands

oudehorne.e: First attested as hoerne cum duabus capellis in 1315. Calque of West Frisian Aldhoarne, derived from a compound of Old Frisian ald (“old”) and hern (“corner of a levee”). The element âld was added to distinguish the settlement from Nieuwehorne.

s.def: a village in Heerenveen, Friesland, Netherlands

oudeschoot.e: First attested as scoeten and scoten in 1299. Calque of West Frisian Aldskoat, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian ald (“old”) and scâta (“piece of land which juts upward”). The element âld was added to distinguish the settlement from Nieuweschoot.

s.def: a village in Heerenveen, Friesland, Netherlands

oranjewoud.e: Attested as het Oranjewoud in 1840. Compound of Oranje (a surname) and woud (“wood”). Named after a manor built by princess Albertina of Orange around 1665.

oldeberkoop.e:
First attested as brokope in 1232-1233. Potentially derived from a compound of Old Frisian ald (“old”), *brôk (“marsh, marshland”) and kope (“purchase”). The element âld was added to distinguish the settlement from Nijeberkoop.
Compare Benschop, Boskoop, Galecop, Gerverscop, Gieltjesdorp, Hei- en...

s.def: a village in Weststellingwerf, Friesland, Netherlands

oosterstreek.e: First attested as Oosterstreek in 1974. From ooster- (“eastern”) + streek (“row of houses”).

s.def: a hamlet in Weststellingwerf, Friesland, Netherlands

overburen.e: First attested as Over Buiren in 1718. Calque of West Frisian Oerbuorren, a compound of oer (“over”) and the plural form of buer (“house, cottage”).

s.def: a village in Weststellingwerf, Friesland, Netherlands

oldeholtpade.e: First attested as holenpathe juxta liennam in 1204. Compound of oud (“old”), holt (“rising deciduous forest”) and Old Dutch pade (“swamp, marsh”). The element oud was added to distinguish the settlement from Nijeholtpade.

s.def: a village in Weststellingwerf, Friesland, Netherlands

oldeholtwolde.e: First attested as oldeholtwolt in 1320. Compound of oud (“old”), either holt (“rising deciduous forest”) or dialectal hol (“low-lying”) and wold (“marsh forest, swamp forest”). The element oud was added to distinguish the settlement from Nijeholtwolde.

s.def: a village in Weststellingwerf, Friesland, Netherlands

oldelamer.e: First attested as oldenlameren in 1320. Compound of oud (“old”) and potentially the hydronym Lemmer or an otherwise unknown toponym Lamer. The element oud was added to distinguish the settlement from Nijelamer.

s.def: a village in Weststellingwerf, Friesland, Netherlands

oldetrijne.e: First attested as ostrinde in 1320. Compound of oud (“old”) and Old Frisian trind (“round”). The toponym may derive from the circular shape of the initial village. The element oud was added to distinguish the settlement from Nijetrijne.

oldegalileën.e: Compound of oud (“old”) and Galilea (“Galilee”). Named after a former monastery.

s.def: a village in De Fryske Marren, Friesland, Netherlands

oudehaske.e: First attested as hasca in 1315. Borrowed from West Frisian Aldehaske, probably derived from a compound of Old Frisian ald (“old”) and *haske (“a species of grass”). The element âld was added to distinguish the settlement from Nijehaske.

s.def: a village in De Fryske Marren, Friesland, Netherlands

oudemirdum.e: First attested as mordum in 1245. Derived from a compound of oud (“old”) and presumably a compound of Proto-Germanic *mari (“lake”) suffixed with the collectivising suffix *-iþi. The element âld was added to distinguish the settlement from Nijemirdum.

s.def: a village in De Fryske Marren, Friesland, Netherlands

oldeouwer.e: First attested as antiqua huer in the 13th century. Borrowed from West Frisian Alde Ouwer, derived in turn from Old Frisian ald (“old”) and ower (“bank, strip of land next to a watercourse”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of De Fryske Marren, Friesland, Netherlands

oosterzee.e: First attested as osterse in 1132. Calque of West Frisian Eastersee, a compound derived from Proto-Germanic *austra- (“eastern”) and *saiwi (“sea, lake”).

s.def: a village in De Fryske Marren, Friesland, Netherlands

ouwster-nijega.e: First attested as Oester Nyegae in 1505. Borrowed from West Frisian Ousternijegea, derived from the toponym Ouwer (derived in turn from Old Frisian ower (“bank, strip of land next to a watercourse”)) and a compound of nī (“new”) and gâ (“village, village area”).

ouwsterhaule.e: First attested as haula in the 13th century. Borrowed from West Frisian Ousterhaule, derived from a compound of the toponym Ouwer (derived in turn from Old Frisian ower (“bank, strip of land next to a river”)) and potentially a term cognate to Old English hafola (“head”), referring here to an ele...

s.def: a hamlet in De Fryske Marren, Friesland, Netherlands

onland.e: First attested as Onland ('t) in 1899. Calque of West Frisian It Unlân, derived from ûnlân (“swampland, low-quality land”).

s.def: a village in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

oosterwierum.e: First attested as aesterwerum in 1319. Borrowed from West Frisian Easterwierrum, a compound of Old Frisian aester (“eastern”) and the dative plural form of weer (“artificial mound used for habitation”). The element easter was added to distinguish the settlement from Lutkewierum.

s.def: a village in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

offingawier.e: First attested as vffenwere in the 13th century. Derived from a compound of Old Frisian Offinga (a surname) and wier (“artificial mound used for habitation”). The Dutch form of the toponym was borrowed from an earlier variant of the Frisian name.

s.def: a village in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands
a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

oosthem.e:
* (village) First attested as aesthem in 1453. Calque of West Frisian Easthim, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian āst (“east”) and hēm (“home, settlement”) or hem (“corner of alluvial land, inland polder”). The element east was added to distinguish the settlement from Westhem.
* (haml...

s.def: a village in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

oppenhuizen.e: First attested as upmahusum and upmanhusum in the 13th century. Derived from a compound of Old Frisian upman (“inhabitant of an upstream area, inhabitant of an elevated area”) and the plural form of hūs (“house”). The Dutch form of the toponym was borrowed from an earlier variant of the Frisian n...

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

osingahuizen.e: First attested as Oedsingahuysen in 1543. Calque of West Frisian Osingahuizen, derived from a compound of Old Frisian Osinga (a surname) and the plural form of hūs (“house”).

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

ottenburen.e: First attested as Attenburen and Ottenburen in the 19th century. Calque of West Frisian Ottebuorren, a compound of Otte (a personal name) or Atte and the plural form of buer (“house, cottage”).

s.def: a village in Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

oudebildtzijl.e: First attested as niuwe zijl in 1570. Compound of oud (“old”), the toponym Biltdijk (a levee) and zijl (“discharge sluice”). The element oud was added to distinguish the settlement from Nieuwebildtzijl.

s.def: a village in Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

oosterbierum.e: First attested as asterberen in the 13th century. Borrowed from West Frisian Easterbierrum, a compound of Old Frisian āstar (“eastern”) and the dative plural form of bêre (“barn, house”). The toponym contrasts with Westerbeeren, a village destroyed by flooding in 1322.

s.def: a village in Goeree-Overflakkee, South Holland, Netherlands

oude-tonge.e:
First attested as die tonghe in 1420-1421. Derived from a compound of oud (“old”) and Middle Dutch tonge (“headland, spit”). The element oud was added to distinguish the settlement from Nieuwe-Tonge.
See also Zealandic Ouwe Tonge.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Goeree-Overflakkee, South Holland, Netherlands

ooltgensplaat.e:
Attested as Oitkensplate in 1554. Compound of Middle Dutch Oltken (a personal name) and plaat (“sandbar visible at low tide”).
See also Zealandic De Plaete.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Goeree-Overflakkee, South Holland, Netherlands

ouddorp.e:
First attested as Outdorp in Westvoirne in 1551. Compound of oud (“old”) and dorp (“village, settlement”).
See also Zealandic Ouwdurp.

s.def: a former municipality of South Holland, Netherlands

oostflakkee.e: Blend of oost (“east-, eastern”) + Overflakkee. The municipality was located on the eastern half of the former island of Overflakkee.

s.def: a former municipality of South Holland, Netherlands
a village and former municipality of Voorne aan Zee, South Holland, Netherlands

oostvoorne.e:
First attested as ostforne in 1260. Compound of oost (“east-, eastern”) and the toponym Voorne.
See also Zealandic Svore.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Voorne aan Zee, South Holland, Netherlands
a village and former municipality of Voorne aan Zee, South Holland, Netherlands

oudenhoorn.e: First attested as den hoorn in 1356. Compound of Middle Dutch out (“old”) and horn (“protruding bend, corner”). The toponym contrasts with Nieuwenhoorn.

s.def: a former municipality of South Holland, Netherlands

oude en nieuwe struiten.e: Derived from oud (“old”), nieuw (“new”) and the plural form of a dialectal variant of Middle Dutch stroet (“marshland overgrown with brushwood”).

s.def: a hamlet in Albrandswaard, South Holland, Netherlands

oude koedood.e: First attested as De Coedoit in 1573. Derived from oud (“old”) and the hydronym Koedood, potentially derived from a compound of koe (“cow”) and dood (“dead”); the river developed as a result of a major flood in 1373, and the hydronym may refer to cattle killed by said flood.

s.def: a former municipality of South Holland, Netherlands

oost-barendrecht.e: Compound of oost (“east-, eastern”) and the toponym Barendrecht.

s.def: a village in Ridderkerk, South Holland, Netherlands

oostendam.e: Potentially attested as totten cleynen waldammen toe, die men heet op desen voorsz. ty dappers - dam in 1435, attested with certainty as Oostendam in 1846. Compound of oost (“east-, eastern”) and dam (“dam”).

s.def: a hamlet in Ridderkerk, South Holland, Netherlands

oude-molen.e: Compound of oud (“old”) and molen (“mill”). Likely named after a mill demolished in 1930.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Hoeksche Waard, South Holland, Netherlands

oud-beijerland.e: Attested as Die gorsse van Egmont Beijerlant in 1546. Compound of oud (“old”) and a compound of Beieren (“Bavaria”) and land (“land”). The village is located in a polder named after Sabina, Duchess of Bavaria, the wife of the polder's sponsor. The element oud was added to distinguish the settleme...

s.def: a village in Schagen, North Holland, Netherlands
a hamlet in Hoeksche Waard, South Holland, Netherlands

oudesluis.e:
* (Noord-Holland) Attested as Duycker Sluys around 1660. Compound of oud (“old”) and sluis (“lock, sluice”). The element oud was added to distinguish the settlement from Nieuwesluis.
* (Zuid-Holland) Compound of oud (“old”) and sluis (“lock, sluice”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Dordrecht, South Holland, Netherlands

oud-krispijn.e: Compound of oud (“old”) and the toponym Krispijn. Named after a former estate owned by nobleman Crispijn van Outgaerden.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Sliedrecht, South Holland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Vlaardingen, South Holland, Netherlands

oostwijk.e: Both compounds of west (“west-, western”) and wijk (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The toponyms contrast with Westwijk.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Molenlanden, South Holland, Netherlands

ottoland.e: First attested as ottelant and ottelandt in 1366. Compound of Middle Dutch Otte (a personal name) and lant (“land, polderland”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Molenlanden, South Holland, Netherlands

oud-alblas.e: First attested as alblas in 1200. Compound of Middle Dutch out (“old”) and the hydronym Alblas, of unclear origins. Compare Merksplas.

s.def: a hamlet in Molenlanden, South Holland, Netherlands

overslingeland.e: First attested as sclinclant in 1263. Compound of over (“upper, upriver”), Middle Dutch slinc (“left, leftward”) and lant (“land”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Krimpenerwaard, South Holland, Netherlands

ouderkerk aan den ijssel.e: First attested as oudekerke in 1263. Compound of Middle Dutch out (“old”), kerk (“church”) and the hydronym IJssel. The toponym contrasts with Nieuwerkerk aan den IJssel.

s.def: a hamlet in Krimpenerwaard, South Holland, Netherlands
a former municipality of South Holland, Netherlands

ouderkerk.e: Named after Ouderkerk aan den IJssel.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Capelle aan den IJssel, South Holland, Netherlands

oostgaarde.e: Compound of oost (“east-, eastern”) and gaarde (“garden”).

s.def: a hamlet in Zuidplas, South Holland, Netherlands

oud verlaat.e: Attested as Oud Verlaat in 1839-1859. Derived from oud (“old”) and verlaat (“small lift-lock”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Zoetermeer, South Holland, Netherlands
a hamlet in Pijnacker-Nootdorp, South Holland, Netherlands

oude leede.e: Potentially attested as lede in 1199, attested with certainty as die lede in 1280-1287. Derived from oud (“old”) and Middle Dutch lede (“excavated or redirected watercourse, especially for marsh drainage”).

s.def: a former municipality of South Holland, Netherlands

oud- en nieuw-mathenesse.e: Named after the fiefdoms Oud-Mathenesse and Nieuw-Mathenesse.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Rotterdam, South Holland, Netherlands

oude westen.e: Derived from oud (“old”) and westen (“west”). The toponym contrasts with Nieuwe Westen.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Rotterdam, South Holland, Netherlands

oud-charlois.e: Compound of oud (“old”) and the toponym Charlois.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Rotterdam, South Holland, Netherlands

oud-mathenesse.e: First attested as matenesse in 1250. Compound of oud (“old”), a variant of Middle Dutch made (“pasture”) and nesse (“headland, spit”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Rotterdam, South Holland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood and former municipality of Rotterdam, South Holland, Netherlands

overschie.e: First attested as skie in the first half of the 11th century. Compound of an inflected form of Middle Dutch out (“old”) and the hydronym Schie. The first element was gradually reinterpreted to over (“over, on the other side of”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Rotterdam, South Holland, Netherlands

ommoord.e: First attested as ommoorden in 1320. Compound of Middle Dutch omme (“around”) and oert (“land protruding into or bordering water”). Originally the name of a polder.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Rotterdam, South Holland, Netherlands
a hamlet in Westland, South Holland, Netherlands

oostbuurt.e: First attested as ostburon in 918-948. Compound of Old Dutch ost (“east-, eastern”) and bur (“house, cottage”). The second element was gradually replaced by buurt (“hamlet”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of The Hague, South Holland, Netherlands

ockenburgh.e: First attested as te ockenberghe in 1280-1287. Compound of Middle Dutch Okke (a personal name) or oke (“place where crops have been planted”) and berch (“hill, elevated place”). The second element was gradually reinterpreted to burg (“fortified settlement, castle”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Wassenaar, South Holland, Netherlands

oostdorp.e: First attested as oistdorp in 1327. Compound of Middle Dutch oost (“east-, eastern”) and dorp (“farmstead, village, subsidiary settlement”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Wassenaar, South Holland, Netherlands

oud-wassenaar.e: Compound of oud (“old”) and the toponym Wassenaar. The toponym contrasts with Nieuw-Wassenaar.

s.def: a village and municipality of South Holland, Netherlands

oegstgeest.e: First attested as osgeregest in 918-948. Compound of Old Dutch Osger (a personal name) and gest (“elevated sandy land”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Oegstgeest, South Holland, Netherlands

oudenhof.e: Attested as hoff-brouck in 1615. Compound of an inflected form of oud (“old”) and hof (“homestead, farmstead”). Originally the name of a polder.

s.def: a village in Kaag en Braassem, South Holland, Netherlands

oud-ade.e: First attested as ecclesia de a in 1395-1396. Derived from a hypercorrected spelling variant of the hydronym Oud A, derived from oud (“old”) and aa (“watercourse, river”).

s.def: a hamlet in Kaag en Braassem, South Holland, Netherlands

ofwegen.e: Attested as Offweege in 1639. Likely derived from the plural form of a variant of afweg (“side path, side channel”).

s.def: a neighbourhood and former municipality of Alphen aan den Rijn, South Holland, Netherlands

oudshoorn.e: First attested as ousthorne and houtshorne in 1305. Compound of Middle Dutch *Out (a personal name) and horn (“protruding bend, corner”). The first element was gradually reinterpreted to oud (“old”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Gouda, South Holland, Netherlands
a hamlet and former municipality of Bodegraven-Reeuwijk, South Holland, Netherlands
a hamlet in Stichtse Vecht, Utrecht, Netherlands

oukoop.e:
* (Zuid-Holland) First attested as oudencoep in 1295. Compound of Middle Dutch out (“old”) and cope (“land purchase, concession”).
* (Utrecht) First attested as aldencoep in 1217. Compound of Middle Dutch out (“old”) and cope (“land purchase, concession”).
Compare Benschop, Boskoop, Galecop, Gerv...

s.def: a hamlet in Bodegraven-Reeuwijk, South Holland, Netherlands

oudeweg.e: Attested as De Oude Wech in 1615. Compound of oud (“old”) and weg (“way, road”). Named after a nearby road.

s.def: a hamlet in Bodegraven-Reeuwijk, South Holland, Netherlands

oud-reeuwijk.e: First attested as oude rewijc in 1295-1297. Compound of Middle Dutch out (“old”) and the toponym Reeuwijk, a compound of Middle Dutch ree (“alignment, boundary line, boundary ditch”) and wyc (“farmstead, village, settlement”) or wic (“inlet, side channel”). Named after a set of polders.

s.def: a hamlet in Bodegraven-Reeuwijk, South Holland, Netherlands

oud-bodegraven.e: First attested as die oude bodegrave in 1295-1296. Compound of Middle Dutch out (“old”) and the toponym Bodegraven. Derived from a hydronym which received its name when the name Bodegraven began to be applied to the settlement around it.

s.def: a former municipality of South Holland, Netherlands
a municipality of North Holland, Netherlands

ouder-amstel.e: Compound of an inflected form of oud (“old”) and the hydronym Amstel.

s.def: a village in Ouder-Amstel, North Holland, Netherlands

ouderkerk aan de amstel.e: First attested as ouderkerken in 1308. Compound of an inflected form of Middle Dutch out (“old”) and kerk (“church”), with the hydronym Amstel added as an identifier.

s.def: a hamlet in Diemen, North Holland, Netherlands

oud-diemen.e: First attested as demen in 1226. Compound of oud (“old”) and the toponym Diemen. Potentially derived from an ancient hydronym derived from Proto-Indo-European *amb- (“water”) or from a term meaning "dark, darkness". The element oud was added when the nearby hamlet of Diemerbrug appropriated the n...

s.def: a hamlet in Diemen, North Holland, Netherlands

over-diemen.e: First attested as overdyemen in 1341. Compound of Middle Dutch over (“over, on the other side of”) and the toponym Diemen.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Gooise Meren, North Holland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Laren, North Holland, Netherlands

oostereng.e:
From ooster- (“eastern”) + eng (“farmland by a village”).
* (Gooise Meren) Attested as De Eng in 1839-1859. The toponym contrasts with Middeneng and Westereng.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Huizen, North Holland, Netherlands
a village in Wijdemeren, North Holland, Netherlands

oud-loosdrecht.e: First attested as loesdrecht in 1298. Compound of oud (“old”), Middle Dutch loes (“drainage channel”) and -drecht (“ferry, watercourse”). The toponym contrasts with Nieuw-Loosdrecht.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Wijdemeren, North Holland, Netherlands

overmeer.e: First attested as uueromeri in 918-948. Compound of Old Dutch uver (“over, upstream”) and mere (“lake”). The toponym contrasts with Uitermeer. Formerly an independent hamlet.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Amsterdam, North Holland, Netherlands

oostzanerwerf.e: Attested as Oost Saender Overtoom in 1680. Compound of the toponym Oostzaan and werf (“shipyard”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Amsterdam, North Holland, Netherlands

osdorp.e: First attested as okesthorpe around 1180. Compound of Old Dutch Oke (a personal name) and thorp (“farmstead, village, subsidiary settlement”). Formerly an independent village.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Amsterdam, North Holland, Netherlands

overtoomse veld.e: Compound of the toponym Overtoomse Sluis and veld (“field”). The toponym refers to an area located west of the Overtoomse Sluis bridge.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Amsterdam, North Holland, Netherlands

overtoombuurt.e: Compound of overtoom (“overland boat ramp”) and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). Named after a street.

s.def: a hamlet in Waterland, North Holland, Netherlands

overleek.e: First attested as overleeck in 1412. Compound of Middle Dutch over (“over, on the other side of”) and the hydronym Leek, derived from lake (“natural watercourse”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Waterland, North Holland, Netherlands

ooster ee.e: Attested as Ooster Aa in 1680, originally a hydronym. From ooster- (“eastern”) + aa (“watercourse, river”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Edam-Volendam, North Holland, Netherlands

oosthuizen.e: First attested as asthusa minore in the 12th century. Compound of Old Frisian ast (“east-, eastern”) and the dative plural form of hus (“house”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Purmerend, North Holland, Netherlands

overwhere.e: First attested as alle hooren lande, dat gelegen is over die weer in 1369. Compound of over (“over, on the other side of”) and the hydronym Weer, which is derived from Middle Dutch were (“fish trap, fish weir”). Named after a polder.

s.def: a village in Alkmaar, North Holland, Netherlands

oost-graftdijk.e: Attested as Oostbuert in 1639. Compound of oost (“east-, eastern”), the hydronym Vuile Graft and dijk (“levee”). The village was initially called Oostbuurt (compound of oost (“east-, eastern”) and buurt (“hamlet, small settlement”)). The element oost was added to distinguish the settlement from W...

s.def: a village and former municipality of Alkmaar, North Holland, Netherlands

oterleek.e: First attested as oterleke in 1256. Compound of Old Frisian oter (“outer”) and leek (“natural watercourse”). Originally a hydronym. Compare Medemblik and Oosterleek.

s.def: a neighbourhood and former municipality of Alkmaar, North Holland, Netherlands

oudorp.e: First attested as aldenthorf in the first half of the 11th century. Compound of Old Dutch ald (“old”) and thorp (“farmstead, village, subsidiary settlement”). Formerly an independent village.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Alkmaar, North Holland, Netherlands

oudorperpolder.e: Compound of the toponym Oudorp and polder (“polder”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Alkmaar, North Holland, Netherlands

overdie.e: First attested as overdye in 1396. Compound of Middle Dutch over (“over, on the other side”) and the hydronym Die, derived from a compound of the article de and aa (“river, watercourse”).

s.def: a village in Wormerland, North Holland, Netherlands

oostknollendam.e: Attested as Knollendam in 1529. Compound of oost (“east-, eastern”), the plural form of knol (“extremity of a levee”) and dam (“dam”). The element oost was added to distinguish the village from Westknollendam.

s.def: a village and municipality of North Holland, Netherlands

oostzaan.e: First attested as oestzaenden in 1318. Compound of Middle Dutch oest (“east-, eastern”) and the hydronym Zaan. The toponym contrasts with Westzaan.

s.def: a hamlet in Haarlemmermeer, North Holland, Netherlands

oude meer.e: Attested as De Oude Meer in 1575. Derived from oud (“old”) and meer (“lake”). Named after a former section of the Haarlemmermeer. The toponym contrasts with Nieuwe Meer.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Haarlemmermeer, North Holland, Netherlands

overbos.e: Compound of over (“over”) and bos (“forest”). The names of the neighbourhood's streets end in bos.

s.def: a village in Bloemendaal, North Holland, Netherlands

overveen.e: First attested as Overveen in 1575. Compound of over (“over, on the other side”) (when viewed from Haarlem) and veen (“peat bog, marsh”).

s.def: a hamlet in Heiloo, North Holland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Heemskerk, North Holland, Netherlands

oosterzij.e:
From ooster- (“eastern”) + zij (“side”).
* (Heiloo) Attested as Oosterzij in 1839-1859. The hamlet is located to the east of the road to Alkmaar.
* (Heemskerk) Attested as Oosterzijde in 1664.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Heemskerk, North Holland, Netherlands

onderwijzersbuurt.e: Compound of the plural form of onderwijzer (“educator”) and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after teachers.

s.def: a hamlet in Castricum, North Holland, Netherlands

oosterbuurt.e: Attested as Ooster Buert in 1680. From ooster- (“eastern”) + buurt (“hamlet, small settlement”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Dijk en Waard, North Holland, Netherlands

oudkarspel.e: First attested as aldenkercha in 1094. Compound of Middle Dutch out (“old”) and kercspel (“parish, village with a church”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Koggenland, North Holland, Netherlands

obdam.e: First attested as opdam at the beginning of the 13th century. Compound of Middle Dutch op (“up, elevated”) and dam (“dam”). Compare Uitdam.

s.def: a hamlet in Koggenland, North Holland, Netherlands

obdammerdijk.e: Compound of the toponym Obdam and dijk (“levee”).

s.def: a hamlet in Koggenland, North Holland, Netherlands

oostmijzen.e: Attested as Oostmysen in 1639. Compound of oost (“east-, eastern”) and a word which may derive from or be cognate to Middle Dutch mies (“wetland”). Compare De Meije, Mijdrecht, Mijnden, Misson and Mite.

s.def: a village in Drechterland, North Holland, Netherlands

oosterblokker.e: First attested as oesterbloecwere around 1312. Compound of Middle Dutch ooster (“eastern”), bloc (“farmland enclosed by a ditch or fence”) and were (“enclosed piece of land located between two ditches”).

s.def: a village in Drechterland, North Holland, Netherlands

oosterleek.e: First attested as oesterleke in 1311. Originally a hydronym, derived in turn from a compound of Middle Dutch ooster (“eastern”) and leek (“natural watercourse”). Compare Medemblik and Oterleek.

s.def: a hamlet in Drechterland, North Holland, Netherlands
a hamlet in Drechterland, North Holland, Netherlands

oudijk.e: Attested as Den Ouden Dyck in 1680. Compound of oud (“old”) and dijk (“levee”). Named after a former levee.

s.def: a hamlet in Drechterland, North Holland, Netherlands

oosterwijzend.e: First attested as wisende in 1336. From ooster- (“eastern”) and dialectal wijzend (“watercourse”). The element ooster- was added to distinguish the settlement from Westerwijzend. Compare Binnenwijzend, Wijster and Wijzend.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Stede Broec, North Holland, Netherlands
a village in Enkhuizen, North Holland, Netherlands

oosterdijk.e: Attested as Den Ooster Dyck in 1680. From ooster- (“eastern”) + dijk (“levee”). Named after a nearby section of the Westfriese Zeedijk.

s.def: a village in Medemblik, North Holland, Netherlands

onderdijk.e: Attested as Onderdijk in 1839-1859. Derived from onderdijk (“bank at the bottom of a levee”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Medemblik, North Holland, Netherlands
synonym of Opperdoezer Ronde (“a variety of potato”)

opperdoes.e: First attested as those and thosa at the end of the 11th century. Compound of opper (“upper, landinward”) and Old Dutch thosa (“mossy bog, overgrown swamp or marsh, light turf”).

s.def: a village and municipality of North Holland, Netherlands

opmeer.e: First attested as upmere around 1312. Compound of Middle Dutch up (“up, elevated, upstream”) (potentially used here in the sense "at") and meer (“lake”).

s.def: a village in Hollands Kroon, North Holland, Netherlands

oude niedorp.e: First attested as nienthorp around the end of the 11th century. Derived from Middle Dutch out (“old”) and a compound of nie (“new”) and dorp (“village, secondary settlement”). The element oud was added to distinguish the settlement from Nieuwe Niedorp.

s.def: a hamlet in Hollands Kroon, North Holland, Netherlands

oosterklief.e: Attested as Ooster kliff in 1680. From ooster- (“eastern”) and dialectal klief (“slope, precipice”). The element ooster- was added to distinguish the settlement from Westerklief.

s.def: a former municipality of North Holland, Netherlands
a village in Texel, North Holland, Netherlands

oudeschild.e: Attested as t Schilt in 1618. Compound of oud (“old”) and an unclear element. The second element may derive from West Frisian skil (“small seashell”) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -t or, alternatively, be cognate to the hydronym Schelde. The element oud was added to distinguish the sett...

s.def: a hamlet in Texel, North Holland, Netherlands

ongeren.e: First attested as ungherburen in 1363. Compound of Old Frisian Ungher (a personal name) and the plural form of buer (“cottage, small house”).

s.def: a hamlet in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

oldorp.e: First attested as Aldathorpe in 1064. Compound of Old Frisian ald (“old”) and thorp (“village”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Soest, Utrecht, Netherlands

overhees.e: First attested as Overhese in 1693. Compound of over (“over, on the other side of”) and the toponym Hees.

s.def: a hamlet in Leusden, Utrecht, Netherlands

oud-leusden.e: First attested as lisiduna in 777. Compound of oud (“old”) and the toponym Leusden. The village was initially called Leusden, but came to be known as Oud-Leusden when a neighboring settlement overtook it.

s.def: a hamlet in Utrechtse Heuvelrug, Utrecht, Netherlands

oud rijsenburg.e: Derived from oud (“old”) and the toponym Rijsenburg.

s.def: a hamlet in Wijk bij Duurstede, Utrecht, Netherlands

overlangbroek.e: First attested as langebruke in 1219. Compound of Middle Dutch overe (“upper, upstream”), lanc (“long”) and broec (“marsh, marshland”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Bunnik, Utrecht, Netherlands

odijk.e:
First attested as iodichem [read: i odichem] in 918-948. Etymology uncertain. The toponym appears to be a compound of an unknown first element and the suffix Proto-Germanic *-ikja-. The element Old Dutch hēm (“home, settlement”) in the oldest attestation is a scribal insertion.
Compare Blerick, B...

s.def: a hamlet and former municipality of Houten, Utrecht, Netherlands

oud-wulven.e: First attested as ouden wuolven in 1381-1383. Compound of Middle Dutch out (“old”) and the toponym Wulven.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Houten, Utrecht, Netherlands

overdam.e: Potentially attested as overdamme in 1263, attested with certainty as overdam in 1357. Compound of Middle Dutch oever (“over, on the other side of”) and dam (“dam”). Named after a farmhouse located on the other side of a ditch when seen from Loerik.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Nieuwegein, Utrecht, Netherlands

oudegein.e: First attested as theloneum ... geyn and theloneo in geyn in 1200. Compound of Middle Dutch out (“old”) and the hydronym Gein, possibly derived in turn from Proto-Germanic *gainon (“yawn, gape”). Compare Gein.

s.def: a neighbourhood of IJsselstein, Utrecht, Netherlands

overwaard.e: Compound of over (“upper, upstream”) and waard (“land next to water, riverine island, polder complex surrounded by water”). Named after a street, named in turn after a former brick factory, named in turn after a polder.

s.def: a hamlet in Vijfheerenlanden, Utrecht, Netherlands

ossenwaard.e: Compound of the plural form of os (“ox”) and waard (“land next to water, riverine island, polder complex surrounded by water”).

s.def: a hamlet in Vijfheerenlanden, Utrecht, Netherlands

overboeicop.e:
Attested as Over Boeicop in 1839-1859. Compound of over (“upper, upstream”) and the toponym Boeicop, for which see Hei- en Boeicop.
Compare Benschop, Boskoop, Galecop, Gerverscop, Gieltjesdorp, Hekendorp, Hoenkoop, Laag-Nieuwkoop, Middelkoop, Nieuwkoop, Nijeberkoop, Oldeberkoop, Oukoop, Overheico...

s.def: a hamlet in Vijfheerenlanden, Utrecht, Netherlands

overheicop.e:
First attested as over-heycoep in 1418. Compound of over (“upper, upstream”) and the toponym Heicop, for which see Hei- en Boeicop.
Compare Benschop, Boskoop, Galecop, Gerverscop, Gieltjesdorp, Hekendorp, Hoenkoop, Laag-Nieuwkoop, Middelkoop, Nieuwkoop, Nijeberkoop, Oldeberkoop, Oukoop, Overheico...

s.def: a city and municipality of Utrecht, Netherlands

oudewater.e: First attested as ovdewatere in 1239. Compound of Middle Dutch out (“old”) and water (“water”). Originally a hydronym. The hydronym gradually came to be applied to the city; the watercourse it referred to was renamed to Linschoten.

s.def: a hamlet in Woerden, Utrecht, Netherlands

oud-kamerik.e: First attested as oldecamerike in 1296. Compound of Middle Dutch out (“old”) and the toponym Kamerik.

s.def: a former village and former municipality of Utrecht, Netherlands

oudhuizen.e: Compound of oud (“old”) and the plural form of huis (“house”).

s.def: a village in Stichtse Vecht, Utrecht, Netherlands

oud-zuilen.e: First attested as sulen around 1169. Etymology uncertain. Potentially derived from an ancient hydronym. The village was initially called Zuilen before being overshadowed by its younger neighbour, which led to the application of the prefix oud (“old”) to the toponym.

s.def: a hamlet in Stichtse Vecht, Utrecht, Netherlands

oud-aa.e: First attested as lacum qui vocatur a in 1138. Compound of Middle Dutch out (“old”) and the hydronym A, derived from aa (“watercourse, river”). The element oud was added to distinguish the settlement from Nieuwer Ter Aa.

s.def: a hamlet in Stichtse Vecht, Utrecht, Netherlands

oud-maarsseveen.e: Attested sometime after 1641. Compound of oud (“old”) and the toponym Maarsseveen.

s.def: a hamlet in Stichtse Vecht, Utrecht, Netherlands

oud over.e: Derived from oud (“old”) and over (“across, over”). The hamlet derives its name from its location on the other side of the Vecht when seen from Loenen.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Stichtse Vecht, Utrecht, Netherlands

op buuren.e: Attested as OPBUUREN in 1846. Derived from Opbueren (a surname). Named after a former manor.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Utrecht, Utrecht, Netherlands

oog in al.e: Attested as Oog in al in 1743. Derived from oog (“eye”) and al (“all”). Named after a former manor, the name of which is thought to have referred to the desire of its owner, aristocrat Everard Meyster, to oversee the grandiose expansion projects for Utrecht he conceptualised.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Utrecht, Utrecht, Netherlands

overvecht.e: Compound of over (“over, across”) and the hydronym Vecht.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Utrecht, Utrecht, Netherlands

oudwijk.e: First attested as aldewic in 1156. Compound of Old Dutch alt (“old”) and *wīc (“farmstead, village, settlement”). By surface analysis, oud + wijk.

s.def: a hamlet in Utrecht, Utrecht, Netherlands

ockhuizen.e: First attested as toechusen in 1280-1287. Compound of Middle Dutch ooc (“smaller piece of land belonging to but separated from a larger area”) or oke (“place where crops have been planted, accreted land”) and the plural form of huus (“house”).

s.def: a hamlet and former municipality of Utrecht, Utrecht, Netherlands

oudenrijn.e: First attested as den ouden ryn in 1498. Named after the Oude Rijn river.

s.def: to switch (over), to change (over) to reverse, to commute
caption (title or brief explanation attached to an illustration or cartoon)
a place where one is received, (temporarily) accommodated, etc.
a village and former municipality of Maaseik, Belgium

opoeteren.e: First attested as votra in 952. Compound of op (“upper, upstream”) and the dative plural form of the hydronym Oeter. The toponym contrasts with Neeroeteren.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Bree, Belgium

opitter.e: First attested as Iteram in 1143. Compound of op (“upper, upstream”) and the hydronym Itter. The hydronym in turn derives from either Proto-Indo-European *id- (“swell, engorge”) or *ajadh- (“bright, clear”). The prefix op was added at a later date to distinguish the settlement from Neeritter. Com...

oudsbergen.e: Named after a hill located at the centre of the municipalities which fused to form the new municipality.

onmogelijkheid.e: From onmogelijk (“impossible”) + -heid. Compare German Unmöglichkeit, Danish umulighed, Swedish omöjlighet.

oorbaar.e: From Middle Dutch orbare. Equivalent to oor- (“prefix meaning "original"”) + baren (“to carry”).

ov-fiets.e: Literally, “public transport bike”; compound of ov + fiets.

onmens.e: 17th century, from on- (“un-”) + mens (“human being”), possibly after German Unmensch.

ossewit.e: Compound of ossen (“of oxen or cattle”) and wit (“white substance”)

examples: Een overnaads gebouwd schip.

overnaads.e: Probably from over + naad + -s, and not from overnaad because use of the noun is attested much later. The adverb is older than the adjective (contrary to the WNT's inclusion of only the latter).

to come round, to change one's mind, to concede, to change one's tune, to change tack

overstag gaan.e: Overstag is a univerbation of over + stag.

op staande voet.e: Calque of Medieval Latin stante pede (literally “with one’s foot standing still”), a medieval juridical term meaning "on the spot, without having left the court". Cognate with German stehenden Fußes. Compare Old Novgorodian како стоꙗ (kako stoja).

ongebreideld.e: From on- + gebreideld, past participle of breidelen (“to bridle”).

onversaagd.e: From on- + versaagd, past participle of versagen (“to lose heart”).

of one's own accord, on one's own initiative

examples: Veel kinderen weten niet wat er van hun verwacht wordt wanneer ze huiswerk opkrijgen.

examples: Nederland telt 5 miljoen 'kleine eters' die porties niet opkrijgen.

ortolaan.e: From French ortolan, from Italian ortolano, from Latin hortulānus.

s.def: to defragment (ecologically), used of habitats or landscapes

ontsnipperen.e: From snipper + ont- -en; a fairly recent coinage modeled on its antonym versnipperen.

oorkwal.e: From oor (“ear”) + kwal (“jellyfish”), named after its visible and (somewhat) ear-shaped gonads.

examples: Het shirt ligt in de zon op te drogen.

opzoomer.e: From Bergen op Zoom + -er, a city in the Netherlands.

overanalyseren.e: From over- + analyseren, possibly a calque of English overanalyze.

op voet van gelijkheid.e: Compare French sur un pied d'égalité.

op voet van oorlog.e: Compare French sur le pied de guerre.

onwijs.e: From Middle Dutch onwijs (“unwise”). Equivalent to on- + wijs. The sense of "incredible" became popular in youth-speak around 1980.

examples: In sommige landen is er een opkomstplicht voor verkiezingen.

to contend with effort [with tegen ‘with’], to act with exertion

ooievaarsbek.e: From ooievaar (“stork”) + -s- + bek (“animal's mouth, beak, bill”). The name refers to the fruit capsule of many species of this genus, which somewhat resembles a stork's bill in shape (see picture). Compare reigersbek (“erodium, storksbill”), which has similar elongated fruits and is similarly n...

s.def: a member of an emergency service, particularly the police, fire and ambulance services, who coordinates the on-site deployment of their service during a large-scale emergency

onder de wol.e: Literally, “under the wool”, referring to a woolen blanket.

examples: Op Curaçao loopt op veel plekken ongezuiverd rioolwater de zee in.
Vroeger kregen we B12 binnen door ongezuiverd water te drinken en ongewassen groenten en fruit te eten.
Bij economische neergang zijn ongediplomeerde werknemers vaak de eersten die ontslag krijgen.

om de tuin leiden.e: Compare English lead someone up the garden path.

ooftboom.e: Compound of ooft (“fruit”) + boom (“tree”). Cognate with German Obstbaum.

examples: op hete kolen zitten

open day (casual event where an institution is open for inspection by anyone interested)

opendeurdag.e: From open deur + dag. Compare French journée portes ouvertes.

s.def: a sensitive, painful issue; raw nerve
examples: De oorlog blijft een open zenuw in het publieke debat.
De opmerking van de politicus raakte een open zenuw.

op eigen benen staan.e: Compare German auf eigenen Beinen stehen.

oorverdovend.e: Compound of oor (“ear”) + verdovend (“deafening”). Compare German ohrenbetäubend.

obstructie.e: Borrowed from French obstruction and Latin obstructiō.

obstrueren.e: 17th century. Borrowed from Latin obstruō, obstruere and French obstruer.

s.def: frank, direct, honest; without adornment or obfuscation

ongezouten.e: From on- + gezouten, past participle of zouten (“to salt”).

examples: Dit product is momenteel onleverbaar.

omwereld.e: Calque of German Umwelt, equivalent to om- + wereld. Compare English umbworld, Swedish omvärld, Danish omverden.

s.def: clear, undisguised, without concealment, not sugarcoated

examples: Het zit vol met ongure types in die buurt.

onguur.e:
From Middle Dutch ongehiere, from Old Dutch *ungihiuri, from Proto-West Germanic *ungahiurī (see also *unhiurī). The unprefixed form of this word still existed in Middle Dutch as ghehure, meaning "pleasant". Compare German ungeheuer.
Possibly related to eunjer (“wizard”).

to be utterly spent, to be exhausted

examples: Pleur op joh, halvegare.

ov-chipkaart.e: Genericized trademark from OV-chipkaart, equivalent to ov (“public transport”) + chipkaart (“smart card, chipcard”).

onbewust.e: Inherited from Middle Dutch onbewist. Equivalent to on- + bewust

op een oude fiets moet je het leren.e: Literally, “you've got to learn it on an old bike”.

ossenpikker.e: From os (“ox”) + -en- + pikken (“to peck”) + -er, likely a calque of English oxpecker.

s.def: in the southern Netherlands or southern Low Countries in general; to the south of the Rhine and Meuse and their distributaries, which separate the Catholic southern provinces from the northern, largely Protestant parts of the Low Countries

onder de rivieren.e: Literally, “below the rivers”, that is the Rhine and Meuse and their distributaries, which as part of the Rhine-Meuse-Scheldt delta form a natural border between two larger religiocultural regions of the Low Countries (with the Rivierengebied forming a transitional region).

onbetekenend.e: From on- + betekenend, present participle of betekenen (“to mean, signify”).

omverlopen.e: From omver (“knocked over, fallen over”) + lopen (“to walk”).

examples: opwarming van de aarde

to make a hole by walking (in a shoe)
to proceed in a suitable manner

overhooplopen.e: From overhoop (“upside down, overturned”) + lopen (“to walk”).

opzijlopen.e: From opzij (“aside, to the side”) + lopen (“to walk”).

ontademen.e: From ont- + ademen (“to breathe”) or adem (“breath”) + ont- -en.

s.def: to take fear away, to relieve of fear
to take fear away, to relieve of fear

ontangsten.e: From ont- + angsten (“to fear”) or angst (“fear”) + ont- -en.

s.def: to remove an anchor plate (of a building)

ontankeren.e: Inherited from Middle Dutch ontankeren. Equivalent to ont- + ankeren (“to anchor”) or anker (“anchor”) + ont- -en.

s.def: to lose daylight, to become dark, become dusk

ontzingen.e: Inherited from Middle Dutch ontsingen. Equivalent to ont- (“privative suffix”) + zingen (“to sing”).

s.def: to unrivet, to loosen that which has been riveted

ontklinken.e: Inherited from Middle Dutch ontclinken. Equivalent to ont- (“privative suffix”) + klinken (“to rivet”).

s.def: to remove a load (of a ship)

ontballasten.e: From ont- (“privative prefix”) + ballasten (“to load”) or ballast (“load”) + ont- -en (“privative circumfix”).

ontbanen.e: From ont- (“privative prefix”) + banen (“to make way”) or baan (“road, path”) + ont- -en (“privative circumfix”).

s.def: to unbar, remove a bar to unlock a door

ontbeiden.e: Inherited from Middle Dutch ontbeiden. Equivalent to ont- + beiden (“to wait”).

s.def: to make free of (mental) images or representations

ontbeelden.e: Inherited from Middle Dutch ontbēelden. Equivalent to ont- + beelden (“to picture, imagine”) or beeld (“image”) + ont- -en.

ontbasten.e: Inherited from Middle Dutch ontbasten. Equivalent to bast (“bark”) + ont- -en (“privative circumfix”).

s.def: to break loose, start to burst
to emerge from something that is bursting

ontbarsten.e: From ont- (“inchoative prefix”) + barsten (“to burst”). Compare Middle Dutch ontbersten (“to elude, to lack”) which was likely formed with the non-inchoative meaning of the prefix ont-.

ontbeurzen.e: Inherited from Middle Dutch ontborsen. Equivalent to ont- + beurzen (“to put in one's purse (Southern)”) or beurs (“purse”) + ont- -en.

s.def: to remove the leaves from a plant, to exfoliate

ontbladen.e: Inherited from Middle Dutch ontblāden. Equivalent to blad (“leaf”) + ont- -en (“privative circumfix”).

op is op.e: From op in the sense of "used up, gone", thus "[when it is] gone [it] is gone".

s.def: up, all gone, no more, finished
examples: Het snoep is opperdepop.

ontharsen.e: From hars (“resin”) + ont- -en (“privative circumfix”) or ont- (“privative prefix”) + harsen (“to cover in resin”).

s.def: to remove the stones from, to destone

ontstenen.e: Inherited from Middle Dutch ontstênen. Equivalent to steen (“stone”) + ont- -en (“privative circumfix”).

s.def: to remove the flowers from, to deflower

ontbloemen.e: Inherited from Middle Dutch ontbloemen. Equivalent to bloem (“flower”) + ont- -en (“privative circumfix”).

s.def: to remove the flowers from, to deflower

ontbloesemen.e: From bloesem (“blossom”) + ont- -en (“privative circumfix”) or ont- (“privative prefix”) + bloesemen (“to blossom, get flowers”).

s.def: to be swept away by a storm

ontstormen.e: From storm (“storm”) + ont- -en (“privative circumfix”) or ont- (“privative preffix”) + stormen (“to storm”).

ontgisten.e: From gist (“yeast”) + ont- -en (“privative circumfix”) or ont- (“privative prefix”) + gisten (“to ferment”).

s.def: to remove the yellow colour from

ontgelen.e: From geel (“yellow”) + ont- -en (“privative circumfix”) or ont- (“privative prefix”) + gelen (“to become / make yellow”).

ontfonkeren.e: From ont- (“inchoative prefix”) + fonkeren (“to sparkle”) or ontvonken (“to ignite, begin to spark”) + -eren (“frequentive suffix”).

ontvonkeren.e: From ont- (“inchoative prefix”) + vonkeren (“to sparkle”) or ontvonken (“to ignite, begin to spark”) + -eren (“frequentive suffix”).

s.def: to remove a block that closes a road or track, to unblock

ontnaakten.e: From ont- (“inchoative prefix”) + naakten (“to make naked (obsolete)”).

s.def: to make less far, to remove distance to

ontverren.e: Inherited from Middle Dutch ontverren. Equivalent to ont- (“inchoative prefix”) + verren (“to remove, place far away”) or ont- (“away”) + verren (“to remove, place far away”).

s.def: to remove a stopper for a barrel
to remove fizz, to decarbonate (of a drink)
turning a blind eye, feigning ignorance
examples: De schimmige praktijken werden oogluikend toegestaan.

oogluikend.e: The past participle of obsolete oogluiken, which in contemporary usage only survives as the below adverb.

s.def: to outperform verbally; to run circles around someone in a discussion; to outdebate or outwit

onder de tafel praten.e: Literally, “talk [someone] under the table”. Semantically very different from the identically-formed English talk someone under the table.

examples: De spion bevond zich op glad ijs.

s.def: of, from or related to Overijssel (a province in the eastern Netherlands)
a person from or inhabitant of Overijssel

examples: De cinematografie van oorlogsfilms is buitenmate beïnvloed door de Eerste Wereldoorlog en hooliganisme.

jus in bello, the law of armed conflict
theatre of war (area in which armed conflict occurs)

oepsefloeps.e: Humorous extension of oeps; compare hatseflats and hupseflups.

s.def: unpaid, without compensation, pro bono, unsalaried
examples: Dit is een onbezoldigde opdracht.

onbezoldigd.e: From on- + bezoldigd, past participle of bezoldigen.

ordinair.e: Borrowed from French ordinaire, from Latin ōrdinārius (“regular, orderly”), from ōrdō (“order”).

olrijt.e: Borrowed from English alright, with the orthography humorously adjusted to reflect a hyper-Dutch pronunciation.

s.def: Forms transitive verbs, mostly from adjectives, meaning "to make more (adjective), to (adjective)-ify".
examples: leuk → opleuken
vet → opvetten

s.def: in the shadow of a larger place, especially a settlement or polity
examples: Hij woonde in een huisje in het bos onder de rook van Utrecht.

s.def: to be taken lightly, to be ill-considered, to be handled in an overhasty way
examples: Het besluit om een rechtszaak aan te spannen ging niet over één nacht ijs.
Een subsidie van een dergelijke omvang gaat zelden over één nacht ijs.

over een nacht ijs gaan.e: Literally, “to cross one night's worth of ice”, referring to the unreliability of ice that has only had one night's time to form. Compare English on thin ice.

s.def: to put on clothes or objects that are worn around (such as scarves or bracelets)
examples: Hij deed een das om voordat hij de deur uitging.
Ik wilde net mijn rugzak omdoen.
Oeh, lekker hè, die chips!
Oeh, nou stinkt het nog meer.

oeh.e: An onomatopoeia, related to German uh and English ugh.

singular dependent-clause past indicative of openstaan

examples: Hij staat altijd open voor nieuwe ideeën.
Er zijn nog verschillende vacatures die openstaan.

onder vier ogen.e: Literally, “under four eyes”. The same phrase is used in German unter vier Augen, Danish under fire øjne, and Icelandic undir fjögur augu.

s.def: to opt, to choose (especially regarding a choice of citizenship or formal relations)

opteren.e: Borrowed from French opter, from Latin optāre.

s.def: Tragopogon pratensis subsp. orientalis, a subspecies of Jack-go-to-bed-at-noon
examples: Coordinate term: gele morgenster (“Tragopogon pratensis subsp. pratensis”)

oosterse morgenster.e: Literally, “eastern goat's-beard”, referring to the provenance of the subspecies in eastern Europe and western Asia.

optatie.e: Borrowed from Latin optātiō. Equivalent to opteren + -atie.

over de hele lijn.e: Calque of French sur toute la ligne.

examples: Ik leg maandelijks wat geld opzij.

ordewoord.e: Calque of French mot d'ordre, from orde + woord.

examples: In een onvrije samenleving worden kritische stemmen gesmoord.

onvrij.e: From Middle Dutch onvri; from Old Dutch [Term?], from Proto-West Germanic *unfrī; cognates include Middle High German unfrī (German unfrei), Middle Low German unvrī, Middle English unfre (English unfree). By surface analysis, on- + vrij.

examples: om het even wat
om het even wie

s.def: not praised as fully as deserved (due to great merit); truly excellent, but underrated (even in spite of widespread recognition)

onvolprezen.e: From on- + volprezen, past participle of volprijzen (“to praise fully and extensively”).

s.def: to be given a task, especially a burdensome one
examples: Het andere team kreeg veel meer werk op zijn bord dan wij.
Er is veel werk gaan zitten in de opstelling van deze strategie.
De opstelling van het voetbalteam is niet veranderd.

s.def: to grit one's teeth, to tough it out, to bite the bullet
to get scolded, to receive a talking-to
examples: Hij heeft gister weer op zijn kop gekregen, omdat hij te laat thuiskwam.

opdijk.e: From op den dijk (“upon the dyke”).

examples: Jan haalt uit naar Jos Brink en daarmee naar het Nederlandse publiek, ook zijn publiek. Jan maakt zich zorgen over de produktie die 2 1/2 miljoen gulden (!) heeft gekost en verzucht dan: „Maar wat wil je dan? Iets als die ongein van Jos Brink?"

Eigenlijk zou een psychiater eens moeten uitzoeken, wat Sonja Barend, de schrijvers van dit liedje en de VARA-leiding er toe brachten zóveel verbijsterende ongein te spuien, maar de hele zaak zal hij wel kunnen herleiden tot dezelfde mentaliteit, die sommige pubers ertoe brengt in een kerk een luide wind te laten.
Wij menen reden te hebben niet zonder meer aan te nemen dat het hier een „(on)geintje" betrof, doch het lijkt ons beter dit incident om een dertienjarige niet verder uit te pluizen.
De Marx brothers en de Ritz brothers zijn daar zeer groot in, maar Olsen en Johnson komen vaak lelijk in de buurt van de ongijn.
De kwajongensstreken in de film zijn zo zouteloos en ongeinig, dat je het liefst zou hebben dat de projectietechniek in een bioscoop het mogelijk maakt de boel snel door te draaien.

s.def: to have problems, be in trouble
examples: De louche ondernemer zat omhoog met de belastingdienst.

s.def: synonym of op zijn kop krijgen (“to get scolded, to receive a talking-to”)

examples: Dit meisje wies op in reinheid, tot ze twaalf jaar oud was.
